does: This paper develops a two-stage machine learning system to predict taxi-in routing decisions for landing aircraft at Atlanta's Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport. Stage I predicts which runway exit an arriving aircraft will use, while Stage II predicts whether the aircraft will cross an active departure runway or use an alternative taxiway. The models leverage ASDE-X surface trajectory data combined with aircraft characteristics, destination gates, local traffic rates, and weather conditions across multiple temporal windows. The authors evaluate nine classifiers including Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost using multiple metrics: accuracy, macro-F1 scores, precision-recall, confusion matrices, Brier scores, and calibration error. Results show XGBoost and LightGBM substantially outperform Random Forest, with Stage I achieving 86-89% accuracy and Stage II reaching 70-74% accuracy across different traffic flow conditions.
connects: This work builds on decades of airport surface operations research and air traffic management optimization, extending prior efforts to model controller decision-making in ground routing. It leverages the well-established trajectory data infrastructure (ASDE-X) developed by the FAA for airport monitoring, and applies modern gradient boosting techniques that have proven effective in traffic prediction tasks. The two-stage decision framework mirrors established human factors research on controller workflow and task decomposition. The study responds to growing recognition that airport surface congestion—rather than airspace constraints—limits throughput at major hubs, building on recent work examining taxi-time prediction, runway-occupancy times, and surface conflict detection.
enables: This decision support system can directly augment air traffic controller capabilities during high-traffic periods, providing real-time recommendations that align with existing workflow rather than requiring procedural changes. The modeling approach is transferable to other major airport hubs, potentially enabling standardized surface routing optimization across the National Airspace System. The system's probabilistic outputs and calibration metrics support human-in-the-loop deployment where controllers maintain final authority. Future work could integrate these predictions with automated surface traffic management systems, enable dynamic runway configuration optimization based on predicted exit usage, and feed predictions into departure queuing models to reduce runway crossing conflicts and ground delays.
